title: Candle Wax on Linen
timeEstimate: 20–40 minutes for the conservative path; embedded, dyed, structured, or vintage linen often needs a professional dry cleaner
stainChemistry: Candle wax is a dry-side, wax / oil-family deposit (paraffin, soy, or beeswax, sometimes with added fragrance or dye). Once the bulk is gently lifted off, what often remains on washable linen is a residual oily mark that can be approached with conservative surfactant treatment. Dyed candles can leave a separate pigment shadow that is a different stain class. Linen is a cellulose (flax) fiber; it can tolerate controlled, conservative laundering when the care label allows, but colored linen, structured pieces, and lined / embellished garments need extra caution and often a professional dry cleaner.
whyThisWorks: Letting wax fully cool and harden makes it easier to gently lift the bulk off without smearing it deeper into the weave. A small amount of liquid laundry detergent applied to any residual oily mark provides surfactant action consistent with the conservative oil-stain approach for linen. Cool / lukewarm water per care label and air-drying with inspection prevent residual wax or oil from being heat-set into the fiber.
defaultAssumption: Simple flat linen (napkin, plain shirt) on a washable care label is a Home candidate for a fresh undyed wax drip. Structured, lined, embellished, vintage, dry-clean-only, or unstable-dye linen is a professional case from the start. Embedded, dyed, large, or already-heat-set wax is harder and usually needs a professional dry cleaner.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Read the care label and inspect construction. Structured, lined, embellished, dry-clean-only, vintage, or unstable-dye linen routes to the professional path and stops here. Note candle type (undyed or colored).
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Reject too-soiled or non-colorfast linen per care-label guidance. Do not proceed if the care label disallows wet treatment.

spottingProtocol.agent: Allow wax to cool and lift excess gently
spottingProtocol.instruction: Let the wax fully cool and harden. Use a dull edge to gently lift any solid wax off the weave in flakes. Do not scrape aggressively, gouge the fiber, or grind wax sideways across the linen.
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Minimal mechanical action only. Aggressive scraping spreads wax and can damage the weave. Do not apply heat or solvent at home.

spottingProtocol.agent: Colorfastness test on a hidden seam
spottingProtocol.instruction: Dab a small amount of liquid laundry detergent and cool water on an inside seam using a clean white cloth. Press for one minute and check the cloth for color transfer.
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Stop if dye transfers. Colored or unstable-dye linen routes to the professional path.

spottingProtocol.agent: Surfactant pretreat for residual oily mark
spottingProtocol.instruction: If a residual oily mark remains after the bulk wax is lifted, apply a small amount of liquid laundry detergent directly to the mark. Work it in lightly with a fingertip or soft cloth.
spottingProtocol.dwellTime: Brief dwell per product label, then rinse cool
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Follow the detergent product label. Do not mix cleaning agents.

spottingProtocol.agent: Launder per care label, cool or lukewarm only
spottingProtocol.instruction: Wash on the cool or lukewarm setting permitted by the care label. Rinse thoroughly.
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Do not exceed the temperature limit on the care label; controlled temperatures protect the linen.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Lay flat to air-dry and inspect for any residual wax, oil, or pigment before any heat. If a stain remains, escalate rather than repeat aggressive treatment at home. Do not tumble-dry until the garment is fully clear.
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Heat applied to residual wax or oil sets the stain into the fiber.

steps: 1. Inspect fiber, dye stability, construction, candle type, and care label before any wet treatment.
2. Allow wax to cool; lift bulk gently with a dull edge; minimal mechanical action.
3. Apply pro dry-side / oil-family chemistry appropriate to wax as a controlled product, with flushing and PPE / ventilation per product SDS.
4. Treat any residual pigment from a dyed candle separately, as its own stain class, only with care-label and colorfastness limits in place.
5. Launder cool or lukewarm per care label; do not exceed.
6. Air-dry flat and inspect before any heat. Pro escalation chemistry stays in a controlled environment with PPE / ventilation per product SDS.
products: Cool / lukewarm water per care label
professionalProtocol.temperature: Ambient mechanical lift; care-label cool / lukewarm wash; no heat on residual wax or oil
warnings: Use cool or lukewarm water unless the care label specifically permits warmer washing; avoid hot water for uncertain, colored, structured, or high-value linen
Never tumble-dry linen with residual wax or oil — heat sets the stain
Never use chlorine bleach on colored linen
Structured / lined / embellished / dry-clean-only / vintage linen is a professional case from the start
Do not apply heat to wax in a Home path; pro heat transfer is a controlled professional technique with documented sources and is out of scope here
Confirm colorfastness via hidden-area test before any escalation
Pro dry-side / solvent chemistry requires SDS-driven PPE, ventilation, and flushing

homeSolutions.agent: Care Label, Construction, and Candle Type Check
homeSolutions.instruction: Read the care label. If it says dry-clean-only, or the linen is structured, lined, embellished, or vintage, stop and take it to a professional dry cleaner. Simple flat linen (napkin, plain shirt) on a washable care label can continue. Note whether the candle was undyed (white) or colored — colored wax can leave a separate pigment shadow that is its own stain class.
Wait until the wax has fully hardened. Do not press, rub, or apply heat to soft wax — that drives it deeper into the weave.

Use a dull butter knife or spoon edge to gently lift any solid wax off the linen in flakes. Do not scrape aggressively or grind the wax sideways across the weave — that spreads it. Work outward from the edge of the spot.

On colored linen, dab a little liquid laundry detergent and cool water on an inside seam with a clean white cloth. Press one minute. If color transfers to the cloth, stop and take to a professional. White linen can skip this step.

homeSolutions.agent: Apply Liquid Laundry Detergent to Any Oily Mark
homeSolutions.instruction: If a residual oily mark is left after the bulk wax is lifted, put a small amount of mild liquid laundry detergent on that mark. Work it in gently with your fingertip or a soft cloth — do not scrub.

homeSolutions.agent: Cool or Lukewarm Wash and Air-Dry Flat
homeSolutions.instruction: Wash the linen on the cool or lukewarm setting your care label allows. Rinse thoroughly. Lay flat to air-dry and look closely for any remaining wax, oily mark, or pigment before any heat. Do not tumble-dry until the linen is fully clear.

Heat-transfer methods (irons through paper), freezing protocols, and Home solvents are not in scope for this Home path on linen. If the wax is embedded, dyed, large, already heat-set, or a residual mark / pigment shadow remains after one conservative pass, take the garment to a professional dry cleaner. Tell them: "Candle wax on [simple / colored] linen — [undyed / colored] candle, age [fresh / older], home-treated with cool-down, gentle lift, liquid laundry detergent on residual mark, cool care-label wash, air-dried flat. [Wax cleared / residual oily mark / pigment shadow]."

steps: 1. Read care label and construction; escalate dry-clean-only / structured / lined / embellished / vintage to a pro. Note candle type.
2. Let wax fully cool and harden — do not press, rub, or heat.
3. Lift bulk wax gently with a dull edge — do not scrape or grind sideways.
4. On colored linen, hidden-area colorfastness test before detergent.
5. Apply a small amount of liquid laundry detergent to any residual oily mark.
6. Cool or lukewarm wash per care label.
7. Air-dry flat and inspect for residual wax / oil / pigment before any heat.
8. Escalate if it doesn't clear in one conservative pass.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the linen is dry-clean-only, structured, lined, embellished, vintage, or unstable-dye; if the hidden-area test shows color transfer; if the wax is embedded, dyed, large, or has been heat-set; or if a residual mark or pigment shadow remains after one conservative pass. Escalate to a professional dry cleaner in any of those cases.
warnings: Do not apply heat (iron, dryer, hairdryer) to wax in the Home path
Do not use Home solvents (Goo Gone, mineral spirits, rubbing alcohol, dry-spotters) on wax
Do not scrape aggressively or grind wax sideways — spreads the stain
Use cool or lukewarm water unless the care label specifically permits warmer washing; avoid hot water for uncertain, colored, structured, or high-value linen
Do not tumble-dry residual wax or oil — heat sets the stain
Never use chlorine bleach on colored linen
Pro-strength oxidizers, solvents, and dry-side spotters are not Home steps

neverDo: Never apply heat (iron, dryer, hairdryer) to wax in the Home path
Never use Home solvents (Goo Gone, mineral spirits, rubbing alcohol, dry-spotters) on wax
Never tumble-dry a linen garment with residual wax or oil — heat sets the stain
Never use chlorine bleach on colored linen
Never wet-treat structured / lined / embellished / dry-clean-only linen at home
Never apply professional-strength oxidizers, solvents, or dry-side spotters in the Home path
fiberSensitivities: Linen is cellulose (flax); colored linen requires controlled temperature and a colorfastness check
Structured, lined, embellished, or vintage linen warps or fails under wet treatment and routes to a pro
Linen Home path uses cool / lukewarm water unless the care label specifically permits warmer washing
chemicalBoundaries: Home path: gentle mechanical lift, mild liquid laundry detergent, and water only
No Home solvents, dry-side spotters, peroxide, oxygen bleach, or chlorine bleach as a default Home step on wax
Pro path may use controlled dry-side / oil-family chemistry only when care label permits, colorfastness is confirmed, and SDS-driven PPE / ventilation are in place
homeAllowed: Cool or lukewarm water per care label
Clean white cloth and dull edge for gentle mechanical lift
Air-drying flat and visual inspection before any heat
proOnly: Dry-side / oil-family pro products and oxidizers used in a controlled professional environment with PPE, ventilation, and flushing per product SDS
testFirst: Hidden-area colorfastness test (white cloth on an inside seam) before any detergent dwell on colored linen
flushNeutralizeDry: Rinse thoroughly after the detergent application
Air-dry flat and inspect before any heat
escalationTriggers: Care label is dry-clean-only or restricts wet treatment
Linen is structured / lined / embellished / vintage / high-value
Wax is dyed, large, embedded, or has been heat-set
Residual oily mark or pigment shadow remains after one conservative pass
ppeVentilation: Follow the product label for any concentrate
Gloves and eye protection for any pro-strength chemistry
Ventilate the work area; keep cleaning agents away from children and pets
safetyMatrix.homeVsProBoundary: Home users use only gentle mechanical lift after cool-down, mild liquid laundry detergent on any residual oily mark, and a cool / lukewarm care-label wash. Pro dry-side / wax chemistry is a controlled professional technique and is hidden from the Home path.

materialWarnings: Do not iron / heat-transfer wax at home — pro technique only
Do not freeze / ice or use Home solvents — out of scope for this Home path
Use cool or lukewarm water unless the care label specifically permits warmer washing
Structured / lined linen is a pro case from the start
Dyed candle wax can leave a separate pigment shadow — escalate rather than chase with stronger chemistry at home

escalation.when: Dry-clean-only, structured, lined, embellished, vintage, or unstable-dye linen; wax that is dyed, large, embedded, or already heat-set; colored linen that fails a hidden-area test; or a residual oily mark or pigment shadow that remains after one conservative pass.
escalation.whatToTell: "Candle wax on [simple / structured / colored / vintage] linen — [undyed / colored] candle, age [fresh / older], home-treated with cool-down, gentle lift, liquid laundry detergent on residual mark, cool care-label wash, air-dried flat. [Wax cleared / residual oily mark / pigment shadow]. [Heat or solvent applied? — no]."

contextQuestions: Is the linen plain and washable, or structured / lined / embellished / dry-clean-only?
Was the candle undyed (white) or colored?
How old is the wax — fresh drip or older / set in?
Has the garment been through a hot wash or dryer since the wax was deposited?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Simple flat linen with a washable care label is the only Home candidate. Anything structured, lined, embellished, vintage, or with unstable dye is a professional case from the start. The conservative Home path is gentle mechanical lift after cool-down plus a surfactant on any residual oily mark — heat-transfer and freezing methods are out of scope here. Dyed candle wax leaves a separate pigment shadow class — escalate rather than chase with stronger Home chemistry.

sources.title: University Stain Science (compiled extension reference)
sources.evidence: Oil / fat / wax family responds to surfactant action; conservative unknown-stain fallback (cool water, liquid detergent, rinse, air-dry); blot rather than rub; do not iron or dry stained fabric until removal is confirmed.

sources.title: EPA Alternative Cleaning Curriculum
sources.evidence: Surfactant chemistry for oily soils; heat / chemical concentration / abrasion can damage fabric; test new detergents on inconspicuous areas; follow product labels and PPE / ventilation for concentrates; clean per care label or reject inappropriate garments.

sources.title: GONR Material Safety Matrix v2
sources.evidence: Linen fiber tolerances and limits; controlled temperature for colored linen; chlorine bleach restricted on colored linen; pro dry-side solvent class belongs outside Home unless explicit label / SDS supports consumer use; absolute safety gates including no cleaner-mixing.

sources.title: R.R. Street 4Streets On-the-Spot
sources.evidence: Wax / oil / grease / paint / lipstick / ink are dry-side stain families with pro dry-side workflow.

sources.title: BÜFA DetaProfi Spotting Chart
sources.evidence: Pro dry-side / oil-family products (Lacex / Detasolv) cover grease / oil / resin / wax problems in a professional environment; hem test required for linen and brilliant colors on natural fibers.
